text: I'm Dr. Karen Zaghian. I'm a colorectal surgeon in Los Angeles and part of the teaching faculty at Cedars-Sinai.
Today's case is a laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy for diverticulitis.

Our patient's a sixty-five year old woman with recurrent uncomplicated diverticulitis over the past several years, which has progressively worsened over the past year where she's had five discrete attacks in a period of nine months. Therefore, she's elected to proceed with surgery.

Now, while my routine sigmoid colectomy performed laparoscopically involves a laparoscopic ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, a laparoscopic mobilization of the colon, as well as the splenic flexure, and a laparoscopic distal transection of the rectum, in diverticulitis, this may be tricky due to the significant inflammatory changes we see that are associated with the disease.
The surgeon performs an open distal transection of the rectum to assess the health of the distal rectum during the sigmoidectomy for diverticulitis.

Fluorescence angiography is used to evaluate the vascular blood supply of the proximal colon before performing the anastomosis. A flexible sigmoidoscopy is conducted to air leak test the anastomosis and check for defects, blood supply, and bleeding within the lumen.
The surgeon uses a Veress needle to enter the abdomen, noting the patient's history of an open appendectomy through the right iliac fossa.
The surgeon prepares to perform a laparoscopic sigmoidectomy by inserting a ten millimeter trocar into the umbilicus, initiating the procedure with gas insufflation.
The surgeon inserts a ten millimeter, thirty degree laparoscopic scope through a balloon trocar to ensure stability and enhance visualization during the sigmoidectomy procedure.
The surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity to ensure no injury occurred from the varus needle insertion, confirming the area looks intact before proceeding to place additional trocars.
The assistant places a twelve millimeter trocar in the right lower quadrant, positioned two finger breadths from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) and two finger breadths upwards.
A five millimeter trocar is placed one hand breadth above the previous site, ensuring it is positioned directly across the working area without interfering with other trocars.

The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg position with the left side elevated. The surgeon sweeps the bowel loops out of the pelvis without grabbing them, clearing the surgical field for the pelvic procedure.
The surgeon uses bowel graspers to carefully manipulate the bowel loops, minimizing trauma while positioning them out of the pelvic area.
Now this woman has had uncomplicated diverticulitis. She's never had an abscess. She's just had multiple attacks over a period of several years.
The surgical field shows inflammation and adhesions in the area, indicating the possibility of a previous abscess.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions from the bowel loops, ensuring no injury occurs to the surrounding bowel tissue during the process.
The surgeon examines an inflamed segment of the sigmoid colon, which is visibly reddened and swollen, indicating it will need to be resected.
The surgeon begins the dissection in a medial to lateral fashion, preparing to remove the inflamed segment of the sigmoid colon while carefully navigating around adhesions and bowel loops.
The surgeon locates the sacral promontory and identifies the inferior mesenteric artery as it approaches the sigmoid colon, noting the proximity to the iliac vessels.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, distinguishing it from the iliac branches located below. The sacral promontory is visible in the surgical field.

The assistant holds up the tissue as the surgeon uses shears to score the area just distal to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, identifying the retroperitoneal plane with the iliac vessels visible below.
The surgeon locates the ureter to ensure safe ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, avoiding injury to surrounding retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon switches to a ten millimeter ligature device to perform the dissection, focusing on achieving a secure seal of the vessels during the sigmoidectomy.

The surgeon uses a blunt dissecting tool to develop the retroperitoneal plane, with visible progress in separating tissue layers. The ureter is observed, potentially being pulled into the mesentery during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the ureter within the surgical field, noting its proximity to the sidewall. The retroperitoneal plane appears inflamed and bloodier than usual due to diverticulitis.
The surgeon identifies the ureter, which is visible in the surgical field, confirming that the fat plane above it can be safely swept down.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and sweeps it down along with the retroperitoneum to develop a medial to lateral dissection plane, avoiding the psoas tendon to prevent going too deep into the retroperitoneum. The dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure.
The surgeon uses a ten millimeter ligature to take a portion of the peritoneum, enhancing the lateral dissection and creating more space for further surgical maneuvers.

The surgeon points to the peritoneum and begins to carefully dissect it, creating more space in the surgical field. The dissection is performed without yet addressing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).

The surgeon carefully opens the peritoneum to create a larger working window, taking care to avoid the hypogastric nerves. The procedure is being performed on a female patient, where these nerves are less critical for sexual function.
However, it can still be important. It's definitely important in men. And while many people don't discuss sexual function when they're doing sigmoid dissections and resections for benign

The surgeon identifies the ureter and prepares to ligate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at a lower point on its pedicle, without needing to take it high due to the benign nature of the disease. The nerves may not be visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a triple ligation of the blood vessel, avoiding skeletonization due to benign disease. The ligation is done high to prevent injury to nerves running under and parallel to the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon exposes a bare window distally, highlighting the need to avoid injuring the ligament of Treitz, which may be near the area when taking the pedicle high.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and prepares to ligate it. The surrounding tissue is carefully managed to minimize morbidity, especially in benign disease cases.
The surgeon continues the dissection after dividing the previous structure, carefully locating the ureter to ensure it remains unharmed during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and proceeds with dissection superior to it, aiming to mobilize the tissue for easier lateral dissection across the white line of Toldt.
The surgeon identifies the psoas muscle, indicating that the dissection plane is too deep. The ureter is visible below, and the surgeon adjusts to find the correct plane within the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon identifies a fatty layer of retroperitoneum during dissection, noting the presence of a fusion plane and the unexpected separation of alveolar tissue, which should not be visible in the mesentery.

The surgeon identifies the retroperitoneum and notes that the previous dissection was too deep, revealing the psoas muscle. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) has been taken, and the ureter is located near the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon encounters a meso that is tightly adhered to the retroperitoneum, likely due to inflammation, resulting in a bloodier field than usual. The dissection proceeds laterally, with a sweeping motion from top to bottom to carefully avoid injury to surrounding structures.

The surgeon attempts medial dissection to simplify the lateral work, navigating through potentially sticky or difficult tissue planes. If challenges arise, the plan is to revert to the lateral dissection plane for continued mobilization.

Now, I know that in this woman, I'm going to have to mobilize the splenic flexure. I had a hunch that that was going to be the case based on her preoperative imaging. She actually has a fairly long segment of diverticulitis involving a fifteen centimeter segment of colon. So, looking at the preoperative imaging can often guide. You can, I think, take this. It'll guide what you think you're going to do intraop. Some people routinely take the splenic flexure down for left-sided cases. I routinely take it when I'm doing a rectal cancer case where I know my colon's going to have to reach further down into the pelvis. I think that's enough.

The surgeon mobilizes the dissection plane from below, transitioning from medial to lateral dissection due to the sticky nature of the medial approach. An air bubble is visible, created during the medial dissection, illustrating the effectiveness of the medial-to-lateral technique in this left-sided case.
The surgeon identifies an accessible section of tissue to dissect while the patient is in a head-down position, preparing to reposition her for mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon identifies the ureter beneath inflamed tissue, applying tension to visualize the dissection area. The dissection progresses into the diverticulitis-affected region, where the tissue is adherent to the sidewall, requiring careful manipulation.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and carefully uses electrocautery to dissect tissue that is heavily adhered to the sidewall, avoiding the colon and pelvic vessels. A potential dissection window is evaluated, with the ureter visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the colon and assesses the dissection plane, noting the ureter's medial position. A trocar is inserted to aid in retraction and suction during the dissection, which is challenging due to adhesions. Care is taken to avoid damaging the colon or ureter while dissecting the sidewall.

The surgeon examines a structure that appears to be a band near the ureter, assessing its position relative to the surgical field. The ureter is visualized, and the surgeon considers the safety of proceeding with the dissection, noting that the ureter is likely staying within the wall and not extending into the area of concern.

The surgeon performs careful and blunt dissection in a densely adhered area, avoiding structures that could potentially be the ureter. The tissue is very stuck, and the surgeon considers making a Pfannenstiel incision to continue the dissection openly, which would allow for easier separation of the tissues.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the splenic flexure, carefully pulling the colon over to create space for the dissection. The long segment of inflammation is visible, necessitating additional length for the upcoming anastomosis. The surgeon avoids extending dissection up to the spleen to prevent splenic injuries.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the colon, identifying and taking down a band of tissue to mobilize the splenic flexure. The attachments are pulled and separated to avoid extending dissection towards the spleen. The surgeon considers two approaches to access the lesser sac, either by continuing in the current direction or by taking the omentum and moving downward.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac to continue mobilizing the splenic flexure, carefully avoiding injury to the stomach visible above. The colon is positioned below, and the surgeon identifies a bare area while pulling the stomach upward toward the head.
The surgeon manipulates the colon, assisting in grasping and positioning it to facilitate further mobilization. The colon is visually identified and maneuvered to aid in the dissection process.
The surgeon identifies the lesser sac and proceeds to dissect the remaining attachments, carefully separating the tissue to continue mobilizing the splenic flexure.

The surgeon ensures the colon is positioned down within the lesser sac and continues to mobilize the remaining section. The colon is reflected over, revealing the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), which has been divided closer to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) below.

The surgeon identifies the pancreas located just above the current dissection area in the lesser sac. The ligament of Treitz is visible medial to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The surgeon notes additional tissue that needs to be dissected and removed.
The surgeon grasps and pulls up a section of tissue, questioning whether it is mesentery. After determining there should be no meso present, the surgeon proceeds to remove the tissue.

The surgeon assesses the mobilization of the sigmoid colon and rectum, considering whether to complete the procedure laparoscopically or through a fan and steel incision due to potential inflammation in the area.

The surgeon examines the inflamed sigmoid colon, noting concerns about inflammation extending to the rectum and potentially beyond the peritoneal reflection. The peritoneal reflection is visible on the medial aspect, and the presence of tinea confirms the identification of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon observes the colon folded on itself and attached to the perineal reflection or the vagina. The colon appears inflamed and unhealthy for division, with the tinea possibly beginning to splay. The surgeon requests a medium Alexis retractor to proceed with opening.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic transversus abdominis plane (TAP) block using a StimuQuick needle. The needle is inserted at the anterior axillary line, halfway between the costal margin and the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS). The surgeon injects a weight-based dose of quarter percent bupivacaine with epinephrine and without epinephrine, aiming for two distinct pops to indicate entry into the TAP plane. The injection creates a visible bulge, indicating correct placement within the muscle layer.

The surgeon observes a visible bulge in the abdominal wall, indicating successful placement of the tap block. The patient weighs sixty kilograms, and the surgeon prepares to administer sixty cc's of the anesthetic mixture, distributing fifteen cc's at each injection site on both sides of the abdomen.

The surgeon performs a fan and steel incision, cutting through the subcutaneous tissues to the anterior sheath and exposing the rectus muscle dorsally. Subfascial flaps are mobilized to avoid cutting the rectus muscle, employing a muscle-sparing technique. The pyramidalis muscle is identified and preserved. The rectus muscle is split, and the peritoneum is grasped. The surgeon uses a bovie to cut through, ensuring not to injure the bladder, and checks the position of the Foley balloon.

The surgeon uses a medium-sized Alexis O wound protector, which also serves as a retractor, to expose the surgical field. The peritoneum is visible and can be safely manipulated. The patient is placed in the Trendelenburg position, and the bowel loops are packed away to facilitate open surgical work.

The surgeon observes an inflamed section of the colon while keeping the laparoscopic ports in place. The visual field shows adipose tissue, and a surgical instrument, identified as a curator, is being used to manipulate or assess the area.

The surgeon creates a window onto the rectum, revealing a segment of healthy rectum despite the colon being inflamed. The tinea coli are visible, though the lighting makes it challenging to see clearly.

The surgeon uses a contour stapler with a green load to transect the rectum, ensuring the colon is aligned straight and only healthy rectal tissue is grasped. The tinea coli are visibly splayed, and the tissue feels soft to the touch.

The surgeon ensures the vagina is out of the way before closing the contour stapler on the rectum. The ureter is confirmed to be safe on one side. The stapler is fired, and a ligature is used to address a small amount of mesentery still attached. The colon appears inflamed and bulky, but the stapled area of the rectum feels soft and healthy, indicating suitability for anastomosis with sufficient length.

The surgeon uses an ICG camera to perform fluorescence angiography, assessing the vascularity of the colon's mesentery. The lights are dimmed to observe the fluorescence, revealing a blue coloration indicating adequate blood supply for potential anastomosis. The mesentery appears pink, suggesting good vascular reach for reconnection.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery and uses a ligature to take the remaining mesentery. The procedure involves using a purse-string technique instead of the triple staple method, and the bowel is prepared with mechanical and oral antibiotics.

The surgeon passes off the resected specimen of the sigmoid colon. Using a DeBakey forceps and proline suture, the surgeon identifies and excises a diverticulum from the colon to prevent it from being included in the anastomosis.

The surgeon discusses the decision to make an open incision to staple off the distal rectum due to the inflamed appearance of the rectum. The Echelon Sixty stapler is used to dissect a window onto the healthy, soft, and pliable rectum.

So had I stapled the rectum intracorporeally, at that point, there's a couple of options of what to do next. So you can staple laparoscopically the proximal aspect also, and then place your anvil and suture it in laparoscopically, or if you're doing robotic surgery, you can do that. However, here we were already open And so we're doing this portion open. I did have to make a slightly larger fan and steel incision than I would have had I only extracted the specimen through here rather than doing the distal firing of the stapler. But I don't think it's really going to affect her morbidity. She has a slightly bigger wound, but we did a safe operation. So when you secure down this push string,

The surgeon ensures good apposition and closure of the colotomy opening, checking for the absence of diverticula in the staple line. Mesenteric fat is cleared to prevent it from being included in the staple line. The mesentery is straightened in preparation for the anastomosis. The EEA stapler is positioned, and the spike is brought out and grabbed with a long right angle, ensuring the mesentery remains straight for proper orientation and connection.

The surgeon carefully inserts a 29 EEA stapler through the rectum, ensuring it does not accidentally enter the vagina. The stapler is positioned anteriorly on the staple line, with the mesentery straightened before firing the stapler to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a leak test using FlexSig, irrigating the pelvis with saline while occluding the colon proximally. The anastomosis is submerged underwater, and air is insufflated to check for leaks. The staple line appears hemostatic, well-perfused, and widely patent, with no air bubbles indicating a negative air leak test. The anastomosis is located low on the rectum, approximately ten centimeters from the anus, and the bowel is soft and pliable.
